title: Qualified trait access: parser
analysis: The task adds two new expression forms to the Reify parser: (1) `TypeName::ident` — qualified trait access, and (2) `expr.(TypeName::ident)` — instance-level qualified trait access. The parser is tree-sitter based: grammar.js defines the syntax, tree-sitter generates parser.c, and ts_parser.rs lowers the CST to AST. Currently `::` is not tokenized or used anywhere in the grammar. The `ExprKind` enum in lib.rs has 21 variants; we add 2 more. The lowering code in ts_parser.rs dispatches on tree-sitter node kind strings; we add two new match arms and lowering functions following the established pattern (e.g., `lower_member_access`). Precedence 2 (matching `&&` level) means `::` binds loosely among binary-like operators, but since the right-hand side of `::` is restricted to a bare identifier (not an arbitrary expression), the main effect of precedence 2 is on chaining: `A::B::c` left-associates as `(A::B)::c`, and expressions like `Foo::bar + 1` parse correctly as `(Foo::bar) + 1` because `bar` is consumed as identifier before `+` is considered. Instance qualified access `expr.(Qualified)` uses dot-paren syntax at postfix precedence 8 (same as member_access), with the inner part constrained to be a qualified_access node.

prerequisites.description: Add `qualified_access` grammar rule to grammar.js: `prec.left(2, seq(field('qualifier', $._expression), '::', field('member', $.identifier)))`. Add `$.qualified_access` to `_expression` choice list.

Add `instance_qualified_access` grammar rule to grammar.js: `prec.left(8, seq(field('object', $._expression), '.', '(', field('qualified', $.qualified_access), ')'))`. Add `$.instance_qualified_access` to `_expression` choice list. Update precedence comment block to document `::` at level 2.
Regenerate tree-sitter parser: run `npx tree-sitter generate` in tree-sitter-reify/ to produce updated parser.c, grammar.json, and node-types.json.
Add `QualifiedAccess { qualifier: Box<Expr>, member: String }` variant to ExprKind in lib.rs. Represents `TypeName::ident` (and chained `A::B::c`).
Add `InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: Box<Expr>, qualified: Box<Expr> }` variant to ExprKind in lib.rs. Represents `expr.(TypeName::ident)`. The `qualified` field holds a QualifiedAccess expr.
Create test file crates/reify-syntax/tests/qualified_access_tests.rs with `use reify_syntax::*;` import and `parse_decls` helper following the trait_tests.rs pattern.

steps.description: Write failing test `parse_basic_qualified_access`: parse `structure S { let x = Rigid::mass }` and assert the let value is `ExprKind::QualifiedAccess { qualifier: Ident("Rigid"), member: "mass" }`.

Add `lower_qualified_access` method to ts_parser.rs: extract `qualifier` field (lower as expr) and `member` field (as string), produce QualifiedAccess ExprKind. Add `"qualified_access" => self.lower_qualified_access(node)` case to `lower_expr` match.

Write failing test `parse_chained_qualified_access`: parse `structure S { let x = A::B::c }` and assert left-associative nesting: QualifiedAccess { qualifier: QualifiedAccess { qualifier: Ident("A"), member: "B" }, member: "c" }.
Verify chained qualified access works with the prec.left(2) grammar rule. Should pass without changes since the qualifier field accepts any _expression, including another qualified_access.

Write test `parse_qualified_access_in_binary_expr`: parse `structure S { let x = Rigid::mass + 1 }` and assert the let value is BinOp { op: "+", left: QualifiedAccess(Ident("Rigid"), "mass"), right: NumberLiteral(1.0) }. Validates that `::` with identifier RHS doesn't interfere with arithmetic.
Verify precedence interaction passes. Since the right side of `::` is an identifier (not _expression), `Rigid::mass` is always a complete unit, and `+ 1` applies to the whole qualified_access.

Write failing test `parse_basic_instance_qualified_access`: parse `structure S { let x = motor.(Driveable::torque) }` and assert the let value is `ExprKind::InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: Ident("motor"), qualified: QualifiedAccess(Ident("Driveable"), "torque") }`.

Add `lower_instance_qualified_access` method to ts_parser.rs: extract `object` field (lower as expr) and `qualified` field (lower as expr — will be a QualifiedAccess), produce InstanceQualifiedAccess ExprKind. Add `"instance_qualified_access" => self.lower_instance_qualified_access(node)` to `lower_expr`.

Write test `parse_instance_qualified_with_member_chain`: parse `structure S { let x = self.motor.(Driveable::torque) }` and assert InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: MemberAccess { object: Ident("self"), member: "motor" }, qualified: QualifiedAccess(Ident("Driveable"), "torque") }.

Verify chained member access before instance qualified access works. Should pass since `self.motor` is parsed as member_access (prec 8) and `.(...)` is instance_qualified_access (prec 8, left-associative).

Write test `parse_instance_qualified_with_chained_path`: parse `structure S { let x = obj.(A::B::c) }` and assert InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: Ident("obj"), qualified: QualifiedAccess { qualifier: QualifiedAccess { qualifier: Ident("A"), member: "B" }, member: "c" } }.
Verify nested qualified access inside instance qualified access. Should pass since the inner qualified field lowers recursively.

Write test `parse_distinct_ast_nodes`: parse `structure S { let a = Rigid::mass  let b = obj.(Rigid::mass) }` and assert `a` produces QualifiedAccess while `b` produces InstanceQualifiedAccess — distinct ExprKind discriminants.
Verify distinct node test passes — inherent from using separate ExprKind variants. Final cleanup: ensure all existing tests still pass with `cargo test -p reify-syntax`.

design_decisions.decision: QualifiedAccess uses `qualifier: Box<Expr>` rather than `qualifier: String`
design_decisions.rationale: The task specifies left-associative chaining (`A::B::c`), so the qualifier must accept another QualifiedAccess, not just a bare identifier. Box<Expr> supports this naturally: QualifiedAccess(QualifiedAccess(Ident(A), B), c).

design_decisions.decision: InstanceQualifiedAccess stores `qualified: Box<Expr>` rather than decomposed type_name/member strings
design_decisions.rationale: The inner part of `expr.(TypeName::ident)` is itself a qualified_access that can be chained (`expr.(A::B::c)`). Storing it as Box<Expr> avoids duplicating the QualifiedAccess structure and handles nesting naturally.

design_decisions.decision: Grammar restricts right side of `::` to `$.identifier`, not `$._expression`
design_decisions.rationale: The task syntax `TypeName::ident` implies the member is always a bare identifier. This also avoids ambiguity: `Foo::bar + 1` unambiguously parses as `(Foo::bar) + 1` since `bar` is consumed as identifier, then `+ 1` is a separate binary expression.

design_decisions.decision: Instance qualified access grammar constrains inner part to `$.qualified_access`
design_decisions.rationale: The syntax `expr.(TypeName::ident)` specifically requires a qualified access inside the parentheses. Using `$.qualified_access` instead of `$._expression` prevents nonsensical forms like `expr.(42)` at the grammar level.

design_decisions.decision: Instance qualified access at precedence 8 (postfix level)
design_decisions.rationale: The `expr.(...)` syntax is a postfix operation analogous to member access `expr.member` and index access `expr[i]`. All postfix operations share precedence 8, ensuring consistent left-to-right evaluation: `a.b.(Foo::c)` parses as `(a.b).(Foo::c)`.

design_decisions.decision: Qualified access at precedence 2 as specified by task
design_decisions.rationale: Task explicitly states precedence 2. Since the right side of `::` is restricted to an identifier (not an expression), precedence primarily affects the qualifier side. This is safe for the common use case where qualifiers are identifiers (`Foo::bar`). The compiler can reject non-type qualifiers if arithmetic expressions appear on the left.

reuse.how: Both qualified_access and instance_qualified_access follow the same `prec.left(N, seq(field(...), token, field(...)))` pattern as member_access.
lower_qualified_access follows the same pattern: extract named fields via child_by_field_name, lower sub-expressions, construct ExprKind.
New test file uses identical parse_decls helper: parse source with ModulePath::single, return (declarations, errors).

reuse.what: Expression assertion via nested match on let decl
reuse.how: Tests extract expressions by pattern-matching Declaration::Structure → members[N] as MemberDecl::Let → let_decl.value.kind, then assert on ExprKind variant fields.
All lowering functions use self.span(node) for SourceSpan and self.node_text(node) for extracting text. The new lowering functions reuse these same utilities.
